summary: This is Drew DeVault's November 2020 monthly status update covering several projects. He marks SourceHut's second anniversary as a public alpha, reports progress on his Gemini protocol server (gmnisrv) approaching 1.0, notes that his BARE message encoding format has been submitted as an IETF draft RFC, and caps it off with an obfuscated C program that implements a minimal Wayland compositor rendered in the terminal using braille Unicode characters.
key_points: SourceHut reached its second anniversary as a public alpha, with a dedicated blog post covering the milestone
The Gemini server gmnisrv gained CGI support, regexp routing, and URL rewriting, nearing a 1.0 release
BARE message encoding gained implementations in Elm, Erlang, Java, and Ruby, and was submitted as an IETF draft RFC
DeVault wrote a minimal Wayland compositor in obfuscated C that renders to the terminal using braille characters, requiring only POSIX and a C11 compiler
The Wayland compositor joke doubles as a genuine technical flex — it handles shared memory buffers, xdg-shell, and works without OpenGL or GPU drivers
money_quotes: Greetings, humanoids! Our fleshy vessels have aged by 2.678×10⁶ seconds, and you know what that means: time for another status update!
I wrote a new Wayland server for you. Its only dependencies are a POSIX system and a C11 compiler — and it works with Nvidia GPUs, or even systems without OpenGL support at all.
I have submitted a draft RFC to the IETF for standardization.
We can probably ship gmnisrv 1.0 as soon as the last few bugs are flushed out.
key_insight: DeVault's status updates showcase his breadth — from protocol standardization to Gemini servers to writing a Wayland compositor as an obfuscated C joke — all unified by a commitment to minimal, dependency-free systems software.
